summary:
The Tudor Dynasty (1485–1603 AD) was the royal house that presided over England's transformation from a late-medieval feudal kingdom into an early modern nation-state, overseeing the [[event:2432|English Reformation]], the beginnings of overseas exploration, the Renaissance flowering of English literature and arts, and the consolidation of royal authority that laid foundations for later parliamentary conflict. Founded when Henry VII defeated Richard III at Bosworth Field (1485), ending the Wars of the Roses, the dynasty produced five monarchs: the cautious, consolidating Henry VII; the larger-than-life Henry VIII whose six marriages and religious break with Rome reshaped English life; the brief reigns of Edward VI (Protestant reformation accelerated) and Mary I (Catholic restoration and Protestant martyrdoms); and the long, brilliant reign of [[event:2471|Elizabeth I]], which lent its name to an entire cultural era.

The Tudor achievement rested on several pillars. Henry VII restored royal finances and central authority after decades of civil war. Henry VIII dissolved the monasteries (redistributing a quarter of England's land to the gentry), created the Royal Navy, and through the Acts of Supremacy made the monarch head of the Church of England — a religious-political revolution with centuries of consequences. Under Elizabeth, England developed a distinct Protestant identity, defeated the [[event:2339|Spanish Armada]] (1588), and saw Shakespeare, Marlowe, Spenser, and others create an English literary tradition of lasting brilliance. The Tudors bequeathed to their Stuart successors a centralized monarchy, a national church, a growing Parliament, and an English national identity — all ingredients of both the dynasty's achievements and the later [[event:2327|English Civil War]].
